questions.question: Who was the earliest scientist to attempt a scientific basis for classification of living organisms?
questions.explanation: Aristotle was the earliest to attempt a more scientific basis for classification using simple morphological characters.

questions.question: Aristotle classified plants into which of the following groups?
questions.explanation: Aristotle used simple morphological characters to classify plants into trees, shrubs and herbs.

questions.question: On what basis did Aristotle divide animals into two groups?

options.B: Presence or absence of red blood

questions.explanation: Aristotle divided animals into two groups — those which had red blood and those that did not.

questions.question: The Two Kingdom system of classification was developed during the time of:
questions.explanation: In Linnaeus' time a Two Kingdom system of classification with Plantae and Animalia kingdoms was developed.

questions.question: The Two Kingdom classification consisted of which two kingdoms?
questions.explanation: The Two Kingdom system had Plantae and Animalia kingdoms that included all plants and animals respectively.

questions.question: Which of the following was NOT a drawback of the Two Kingdom classification?

options.A: Did not distinguish between eukaryotes and prokaryotes
options.B: Did not distinguish between unicellular and multicellular organisms
options.C: Did not distinguish between photosynthetic and non-photosynthetic organisms

questions.explanation: The Two Kingdom system included animals in Animalia. Its drawbacks were: no distinction between eukaryotes/prokaryotes, unicellular/multicellular, and photosynthetic/non-photosynthetic.

questions.question: Green algae were placed under which kingdom in the Two Kingdom classification?
questions.explanation: The Two Kingdom classification placed photosynthetic organisms like green algae under Plantae, though it could not distinguish them from non-photosynthetic fungi.

questions.question: Who proposed the Five Kingdom Classification?
questions.explanation: R.H. Whittaker (1969) proposed the Five Kingdom Classification.

questions.question: In which year was the Five Kingdom Classification proposed?
questions.explanation: R.H. Whittaker proposed the Five Kingdom Classification in 1969.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the five kingdoms proposed by Whittaker?
questions.explanation: Whittaker's five kingdoms are Monera, Protista, Fungi, Plantae and Animalia. Archaebacteria is a subgroup within Monera, not a separate kingdom.

questions.question: Which of the following criteria was NOT used by Whittaker for his Five Kingdom Classification?
questions.explanation: Whittaker used cell structure, body organisation, mode of nutrition, reproduction and phylogenetic relationships as criteria, not geographical distribution.

questions.question: The three-domain system divides Kingdom Monera into how many domains?
questions.explanation: The three-domain system divides Kingdom Monera into two domains, leaving the remaining eukaryotic kingdoms in the third domain.

questions.question: The three-domain system results in how many total kingdoms?
questions.explanation: The three-domain system divides Kingdom Monera into two domains plus the remaining eukaryotic kingdoms, resulting in a six kingdom classification.

questions.question: Earlier classification systems grouped bacteria, blue-green algae and fungi together under 'Plants' on the basis of:
questions.explanation: The character that unified earlier 'Plants' kingdom was that all organisms included had a cell wall in their cells.

questions.question: What is the cell wall composition in fungi?
questions.explanation: Fungi have chitin in their cell walls while green plants have cellulosic cell wall.

questions.question: Which organisms were placed together under algae in earlier classification despite being very different?
questions.explanation: In earlier classification, unicellular Chlamydomonas and multicellular Spirogyra were placed together under algae.

questions.question: In Whittaker's classification, which kingdom brought together Chlamydomonas, Chlorella, Paramoecium and Amoeba?
questions.explanation: Kingdom Protista brought together Chlamydomonas, Chlorella (earlier in Algae/Plants) with Paramoecium and Amoeba (earlier in Animals).

questions.question: A classification system that reflects evolutionary relationships is called:
questions.explanation: A classification based on evolutionary relationships is called phylogenetic classification.

questions.question: What is the cell type of organisms in Kingdom Monera?
questions.explanation: Kingdom Monera organisms are prokaryotic — they lack a membrane-bound nucleus.

questions.question: Which kingdom has organisms with no cell wall?
questions.explanation: Kingdom Animalia has organisms whose cells lack cell walls.

questions.question: What is the mode of nutrition in Kingdom Plantae?
questions.explanation: Kingdom Plantae organisms are autotrophic and photosynthetic.

questions.question: Which kingdom has organisms with both autotrophic and heterotrophic modes of nutrition?
questions.explanation: Monera organisms can be autotrophic (chemosynthetic and photosynthetic) and heterotrophic (saprophytic/parasitic).

questions.question: What is the body organisation of Kingdom Fungi?
questions.explanation: Kingdom Fungi has multicellular/loose tissue body organisation.

questions.question: Nuclear membrane is absent in which kingdom?
questions.explanation: Kingdom Monera organisms (prokaryotes) lack a nuclear membrane.

questions.question: Which kingdom has holozoic or saprophytic mode of nutrition?
questions.explanation: Kingdom Animalia has heterotrophic mode of nutrition — holozoic/saprophytic etc.

questions.question: Which are the sole members of Kingdom Monera?
questions.explanation: Bacteria are the sole members of the Kingdom Monera.

questions.question: Bacteria are the most abundant:
questions.explanation: Bacteria are the most abundant micro-organisms.

questions.question: Bacteria that live in extreme habitats like hot springs are called:
questions.explanation: Thermoacidophiles are archaebacteria that live in hot springs.

questions.question: Which shape of bacteria is spherical?
questions.explanation: Coccus (pl.: cocci) is the spherical form of bacteria.

questions.question: Which shape of bacteria is rod-shaped?
questions.explanation: Bacillus (pl.: bacilli) is the rod-shaped form of bacteria.

questions.question: Which shape of bacteria is comma-shaped?
questions.explanation: Vibrio (pl.: vibrio) is the comma-shaped form of bacteria.

questions.question: Which shape of bacteria is spiral?
questions.explanation: Spirillum (pl.: spirilla) is the spiral form of bacteria.

questions.question: Which group of organisms shows the most extensive metabolic diversity?
questions.explanation: Compared to many other organisms, bacteria as a group show the most extensive metabolic diversity.

questions.question: Bacteria that synthesise their own food from inorganic substrates are called:
questions.explanation: Autotrophic bacteria synthesise their own food from inorganic substrates.

questions.question: The vast majority of bacteria are:
questions.explanation: The vast majority of bacteria are heterotrophs — they depend on other organisms or dead organic matter for food.

questions.question: Archaebacteria that live in extremely salty areas are called:
questions.explanation: Halophiles are archaebacteria that live in extreme salty areas.

questions.question: Archaebacteria that live in marshy areas are called:
questions.explanation: Methanogens are archaebacteria present in marshy areas.

questions.question: What feature allows archaebacteria to survive in extreme conditions?

options.C: Presence of chitin in cell wall
options.D: Presence of cellulose in cell wall

questions.explanation: Archaebacteria differ from other bacteria in having a different cell wall structure, which is responsible for their survival in extreme conditions.

questions.question: Methanogens are present in the gut of which animals?
questions.explanation: Methanogens are present in the gut of several ruminant animals such as cows and buffaloes.

questions.question: Methanogens are responsible for the production of which gas from dung of ruminants?
questions.explanation: Methanogens are responsible for the production of methane (biogas) from the dung of ruminant animals.

questions.question: Eubacteria are characterised by the presence of:

options.B: Rigid cell wall and flagellum (if motile)

questions.explanation: Eubacteria are characterised by the presence of a rigid cell wall and if motile, a flagellum.

questions.question: Cyanobacteria are also referred to as:
questions.explanation: Cyanobacteria are also referred to as blue-green algae.

questions.question: Cyanobacteria have which type of chlorophyll similar to green plants?
questions.explanation: Cyanobacteria have chlorophyll a similar to green plants and are photosynthetic autotrophs.

questions.question: Cyanobacterial colonies are generally surrounded by:
questions.explanation: Cyanobacterial colonies are generally surrounded by gelatinous sheath.

questions.question: Cyanobacteria often form blooms in:
questions.explanation: Cyanobacteria often form blooms in polluted water bodies.

questions.question: Specialised cells of cyanobacteria that can fix atmospheric nitrogen are called:
questions.explanation: Some cyanobacteria can fix atmospheric nitrogen in specialised cells called heterocysts.

questions.question: Which of the following cyanobacteria can fix atmospheric nitrogen?
questions.explanation: Nostoc and Anabaena are cyanobacteria that can fix atmospheric nitrogen in heterocysts.

questions.question: Chemosynthetic autotrophic bacteria oxidise inorganic substances and use the released energy for:
questions.explanation: Chemosynthetic autotrophic bacteria oxidise inorganic substances and use the released energy for their ATP production.

questions.question: Which inorganic substances are oxidised by chemosynthetic autotrophic bacteria?
questions.explanation: Chemosynthetic autotrophic bacteria oxidise various inorganic substances such as nitrates, nitrites and ammonia.

questions.question: Chemosynthetic autotrophic bacteria play a great role in recycling which nutrients?
questions.explanation: They play a great role in recycling nutrients like nitrogen, phosphorous, iron and sulphur.

questions.question: Which type of bacteria are most abundant in nature?
questions.explanation: Heterotrophic bacteria are most abundant in nature and the majority are important decomposers.

questions.question: Which of the following is a useful role of heterotrophic bacteria?
questions.explanation: Heterotrophic bacteria are helpful in making curd from milk, production of antibiotics, fixing nitrogen in legume roots.

questions.question: Which of the following diseases is caused by bacteria?
questions.explanation: Cholera, typhoid, tetanus, citrus canker are well known diseases caused by different bacteria.

questions.question: What is the main mode of reproduction in bacteria?
questions.explanation: Bacteria reproduce mainly by fission.

questions.question: Under unfavourable conditions, bacteria produce:
questions.explanation: Sometimes, under unfavourable conditions, bacteria produce spores.

questions.question: Bacterial sexual reproduction involves:

options.B: Primitive type of DNA transfer from one bacterium to another

questions.explanation: Bacteria reproduce by a sort of sexual reproduction by adopting a primitive type of DNA transfer from one bacterium to the other.

questions.question: Mycoplasma are organisms that:
questions.explanation: Mycoplasma are organisms that completely lack a cell wall.

questions.question: Mycoplasma are:
questions.explanation: Mycoplasma are the smallest living cells known and can survive without oxygen.

questions.question: Mycoplasma can survive:
questions.explanation: Mycoplasma are the smallest living cells known and can survive without oxygen.

questions.question: Many mycoplasma are:
questions.explanation: Many mycoplasma are pathogenic in animals and plants.

questions.question: All single-celled eukaryotes are placed under which kingdom?
questions.explanation: All single-celled eukaryotes are placed under Protista.

questions.question: Members of Protista are primarily:
questions.explanation: Members of Protista are primarily aquatic.

questions.question: Kingdom Protista forms a link between:
questions.explanation: Kingdom Protista forms a link with the others dealing with plants, animals and fungi.

questions.question: How do protists reproduce sexually?

options.C: By cell fusion and zygote formation

questions.explanation: Protists reproduce sexually by a process involving cell fusion and zygote formation.

questions.question: Which of the following groups is included under Protista in the NCERT textbook?

options.B: Chrysophytes, Dinoflagellates, Euglenoids, Slime moulds and Protozoans

questions.explanation: Chrysophytes, Dinoflagellates, Euglenoids, Slime moulds and Protozoans are included under Protista.

questions.question: Which group includes diatoms and golden algae?
questions.explanation: Chrysophytes include diatoms and golden algae (desmids).

questions.question: Chrysophytes are found in:

options.C: Fresh water as well as marine environments

questions.explanation: Chrysophytes are found in fresh water as well as in marine environments.

questions.question: Chrysophytes are microscopic and float passively in water currents. They are called:
questions.explanation: Chrysophytes are microscopic and float passively in water currents (plankton).

questions.question: In diatoms, the cell walls form two thin overlapping shells. The walls are embedded with:
questions.explanation: The walls of diatoms are embedded with silica and thus the walls are indestructible.

questions.question: The accumulation of diatom cell wall deposits over billions of years is referred to as:
questions.explanation: The accumulation of diatom cell wall deposits over billions of years is referred to as 'diatomaceous earth'.

questions.question: Diatomaceous earth is used in:

options.B: Polishing and filtration of oils and syrups

questions.explanation: Being gritty, diatomaceous earth is used in polishing, filtration of oils and syrups.

questions.question: Diatoms are the chief 'producers' in:
questions.explanation: Diatoms are the chief 'producers' in the oceans.

questions.question: Dinoflagellates are mostly:
questions.explanation: Dinoflagellates are mostly marine and photosynthetic.

questions.question: The color of dinoflagellates depends on:

options.C: The main pigments present in their cells

questions.explanation: Dinoflagellates appear yellow, green, brown, blue or red depending on the main pigments present in their cells.

questions.question: The cell wall of dinoflagellates has stiff plates of:
questions.explanation: The cell wall of dinoflagellates has stiff cellulose plates on the outer surface.

questions.question: Most dinoflagellates have how many flagella?
questions.explanation: Most dinoflagellates have two flagella; one lies longitudinally and the other transversely in a furrow between the wall plates.

questions.question: Which dinoflagellate causes 'red tides'?
questions.explanation: Red dinoflagellates like Gonyaulax undergo rapid multiplication that makes the sea appear red (red tides).

questions.question: What is the effect of red tides on marine ecosystems?

options.B: Toxins released may kill other marine animals such as fishes

questions.explanation: Toxins released by large numbers of red dinoflagellates may even kill other marine animals such as fishes.

questions.question: Majority of euglenoids are found in:
questions.explanation: Majority of euglenoids are fresh water organisms found in stagnant water.

questions.question: Instead of a cell wall, euglenoids have a protein-rich layer called:
questions.explanation: Instead of a cell wall, euglenoids have a protein rich layer called pellicle which makes their body flexible.

questions.question: How many flagella do euglenoids have?
questions.explanation: Euglenoids have two flagella — a short and a long one.

questions.question: When deprived of sunlight, euglenoids behave like:

options.C: Heterotrophs by predating on other smaller organisms

questions.explanation: When deprived of sunlight, euglenoids behave like heterotrophs by predating on other smaller organisms.

questions.question: The pigments of euglenoids are identical to those present in:
questions.explanation: Interestingly, the pigments of euglenoids are identical to those present in higher plants.

questions.question: An example of euglenoid is:
questions.explanation: Euglena is an example of euglenoid.

questions.question: Slime moulds are:
questions.explanation: Slime moulds are saprophytic protists.

questions.question: The body of slime mould moves along decaying twigs and leaves engulfing:
questions.explanation: The body of slime mould moves along decaying twigs and leaves engulfing organic material.

questions.question: Under suitable conditions, slime moulds form an aggregation called:
questions.explanation: Under suitable conditions, slime moulds form an aggregation called plasmodium which may grow and spread over several feet.

questions.question: During unfavourable conditions, slime mould plasmodium forms:
questions.explanation: During unfavourable conditions, the plasmodium differentiates and forms fruiting bodies bearing spores at their tips.

questions.question: Spores of slime moulds are dispersed by:
questions.explanation: The spores of slime moulds are dispersed by air currents.

questions.question: Spores of slime moulds possess:
questions.explanation: The spores of slime moulds possess true walls and are extremely resistant, surviving for many years.

questions.question: All protozoans are:

options.B: Heterotrophs living as predators or parasites

questions.explanation: All protozoans are heterotrophs and live as predators or parasites.

questions.question: Protozoans are believed to be primitive relatives of:
questions.explanation: Protozoans are believed to be primitive relatives of animals.

questions.question: How many major groups of protozoans are there?
questions.explanation: There are four major groups of protozoans: amoeboid, flagellated, ciliated, and sporozoans.

questions.question: Amoeboid protozoans move and capture their prey by putting out:
questions.explanation: Amoeboid protozoans move and capture their prey by putting out pseudopodia (false feet) as in Amoeba.

questions.question: Marine amoeboid protozoans have on their surface:
questions.explanation: Marine amoeboid forms have silica shells on their surface.

questions.question: Which amoeboid protozoan is a parasite?
questions.explanation: Some amoeboid protozoans such as Entamoeba are parasites.

questions.question: Trypanosoma is an example of which type of protozoan?
questions.explanation: Trypanosoma is an example of flagellated protozoan. The parasitic forms cause diseases such as sleeping sickness.

questions.question: Sleeping sickness is caused by:
questions.explanation: Parasitic flagellated protozoans like Trypanosoma cause diseases such as sleeping sickness.

questions.question: Ciliated protozoans are aquatic and actively moving because of the presence of:
questions.explanation: Ciliated protozoans are aquatic, actively moving organisms because of the presence of thousands of cilia.

questions.question: Ciliated protozoans have a cavity called gullet that opens to:

options.B: The outside of the cell surface

questions.explanation: Ciliated protozoans have a cavity (gullet) that opens to the outside of the cell surface.

questions.question: Paramoecium is an example of which type of protozoan?
questions.explanation: Paramoecium is an example of ciliated protozoan.

questions.question: Sporozoans have an infectious spore-like stage in their:
questions.explanation: Sporozoans include diverse organisms that have an infectious spore-like stage in their life cycle.

questions.question: Plasmodium is the causative organism of:
questions.explanation: Plasmodium (malarial parasite) causes malaria, a disease which has a staggering effect on human population.

questions.question: Plasmodium belongs to which group of protozoans?
questions.explanation: Plasmodium (malarial parasite) is the most notorious sporozoan.

questions.question: Kingdom Fungi constitutes a unique kingdom of:
questions.explanation: The fungi constitute a unique kingdom of heterotrophic organisms.

questions.question: Fungi prefer to grow in:
questions.explanation: Fungi prefer to grow in warm and humid places.

questions.question: With the exception of yeasts, fungi are:
questions.explanation: With the exception of yeasts which are unicellular, fungi are filamentous.

questions.question: The long slender thread-like structures of fungi are called:
questions.explanation: The bodies of fungi consist of long, slender thread-like structures called hyphae.

questions.question: The network of hyphae in fungi is known as:
questions.explanation: The network of hyphae is known as mycelium.

questions.question: Hyphae that are continuous tubes filled with multinucleated cytoplasm are called:
questions.explanation: Some hyphae are continuous tubes filled with multinucleated cytoplasm — these are called coenocytic hyphae.

questions.question: The cell walls of fungi are composed of:
questions.explanation: The cell walls of fungi are composed of chitin and polysaccharides.

questions.question: Fungi that absorb soluble organic matter from dead substrates are called:
questions.explanation: Most fungi are heterotrophic and absorb soluble organic matter from dead substrates and hence are called saprophytes.

questions.question: Fungi that depend on living plants and animals are called:
questions.explanation: Fungi that depend on living plants and animals are called parasites.

questions.question: When fungi live in association with algae, they are called:
questions.explanation: Fungi can live as symbionts — in association with algae as lichens and with roots of higher plants as mycorrhiza.

questions.question: Fungi living in association with roots of higher plants form:
questions.explanation: Fungi live as symbionts with roots of higher plants as mycorrhiza.

questions.question: Vegetative reproduction in fungi takes place by:
questions.explanation: Reproduction in fungi can take place by vegetative means — fragmentation, fission and budding.

questions.question: Asexual reproduction in fungi is by spores called:
questions.explanation: Asexual reproduction in fungi is by spores called conidia or sporangiospores or zoospores.

questions.question: Sexual reproduction in fungi is by:
questions.explanation: Sexual reproduction in fungi is by oospores, ascospores and basidiospores.

questions.question: The various spores in fungi are produced in distinct structures called:
questions.explanation: The various spores are produced in distinct structures called fruiting bodies.

questions.question: Which is the first step of the sexual cycle in fungi?
questions.explanation: The first step is plasmogamy — fusion of protoplasms between two motile or non-motile gametes.

questions.question: Fusion of two nuclei in the sexual cycle of fungi is called:
questions.explanation: Fusion of two nuclei in the sexual cycle of fungi is called karyogamy.

questions.question: After karyogamy in fungi, meiosis in the zygote results in:
questions.explanation: Meiosis in zygote results in haploid spores — this is the third step of the sexual cycle.

questions.question: In ascomycetes and basidiomycetes, an intervening dikaryotic stage (n + n) occurs. This condition is called:
questions.explanation: In ascomycetes and basidiomycetes, an intervening dikaryotic stage (n + n, i.e., two nuclei per cell) occurs; such a condition is called a dikaryon.

questions.question: The basis for the division of Kingdom Fungi into various classes is:

options.C: Morphology of mycelium, mode of spore formation and fruiting bodies

questions.explanation: The morphology of the mycelium, mode of spore formation and fruiting bodies form the basis for the division of the kingdom into various classes.

questions.question: Members of Phycomycetes are found in:

options.B: Aquatic habitats and on decaying wood in moist places, or as obligate parasites

questions.explanation: Members of phycomycetes are found in aquatic habitats and on decaying wood in moist and damp places or as obligate parasites on plants.

questions.question: The mycelium of Phycomycetes is:
questions.explanation: The mycelium of phycomycetes is aseptate and coenocytic.

questions.question: In Phycomycetes, asexual reproduction takes place by:
questions.explanation: In phycomycetes, asexual reproduction takes place by zoospores (motile) or by aplanospores (non-motile).

questions.question: In Phycomycetes, the sexual spore formed by fusion of two gametes is:
questions.explanation: A zygospore is formed by fusion of two gametes in phycomycetes.

questions.question: When gametes in phycomycetes are similar in morphology, this is called:
questions.explanation: When gametes are similar in morphology, it is called isogamous.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of Phycomycetes?
questions.explanation: Mucor, Rhizopus (the bread mould) and Albugo (the parasitic fungi on mustard) are examples of phycomycetes.

questions.question: Albugo is a parasitic fungus found on:
questions.explanation: Albugo is the parasitic fungi on mustard and is an example of phycomycetes.

questions.question: Ascomycetes are commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Ascomycetes are commonly known as sac-fungi.

questions.question: Ascomycetes are mostly:
questions.explanation: Ascomycetes are mostly multicellular (e.g., Penicillium) or rarely unicellular (e.g., yeast/Saccharomyces).

questions.question: The mycelium of Ascomycetes is:
questions.explanation: The mycelium of ascomycetes is branched and septate.

questions.question: The asexual spores of Ascomycetes are called:
questions.explanation: The asexual spores of ascomycetes are conidia produced exogenously on the special mycelium called conidiophores.

questions.question: Conidia are produced exogenously on special mycelium called:
questions.explanation: Conidia are produced exogenously on the special mycelium called conidiophores.

questions.question: Sexual spores of Ascomycetes are called:
questions.explanation: Sexual spores of ascomycetes are called ascospores which are produced endogenously in sac like asci.

questions.question: Ascospores are produced endogenously in sac-like structures called:
questions.explanation: Ascospores are produced endogenously in sac like asci (singular ascus).

questions.question: Asci are arranged in different types of fruiting bodies called:
questions.explanation: Asci are arranged in different types of fruiting bodies called ascocarps.

questions.question: Which organism from Ascomycetes is used extensively in biochemical and genetic work?
questions.explanation: Neurospora is used extensively in biochemical and genetic work.

questions.question: Morels and truffles are examples of which class of fungi?
questions.explanation: Many ascomycetes members like morels and truffles are edible and are considered delicacies.

questions.question: Yeast (Saccharomyces) belongs to which class of fungi?
questions.explanation: Yeast (Saccharomyces) is a unicellular ascomycete.

questions.question: Commonly known forms of Basidiomycetes are:
questions.explanation: Commonly known forms of basidiomycetes are mushrooms, bracket fungi or puffballs.

questions.question: Basidiomycetes that live in living plant bodies as parasites are called:
questions.explanation: Basidiomycetes grow in soil, on logs and tree stumps and in living plant bodies as parasites, e.g., rusts and smuts.

questions.question: The mycelium of Basidiomycetes is:
questions.explanation: The mycelium of basidiomycetes is branched and septate.

questions.question: In Basidiomycetes, plasmogamy is brought about by fusion of:

options.C: Two vegetative or somatic cells of different strains

questions.explanation: In basidiomycetes, sex organs are absent, but plasmogamy is brought about by fusion of two vegetative or somatic cells of different strains or genotypes.

questions.question: The resultant dikaryotic structure in basidiomycetes ultimately gives rise to:
questions.explanation: The resultant dikaryotic structure ultimately gives rise to basidium.

questions.question: Karyogamy and meiosis in basidium produce how many basidiospores?
questions.explanation: Karyogamy and meiosis take place in the basidium producing four basidiospores.

questions.question: Basidiospores are produced on the basidium:
questions.explanation: The basidiospores are exogenously produced on the basidium (pl.: basidia).

questions.question: The basidia are arranged in fruiting bodies called:
questions.explanation: The basidia are arranged in fruiting bodies called basidiocarps.

questions.question: Agaricus (mushroom) belongs to which class of fungi?
questions.explanation: Agaricus (mushroom) is a common member of basidiomycetes.

questions.question: Puccinia is an example of:
questions.explanation: Puccinia (rust fungus) is a common member of basidiomycetes.

questions.question: Ustilago (smut) belongs to which class of fungi?
questions.explanation: Ustilago (smut) is a common member of basidiomycetes.

questions.question: Deuteromycetes are commonly known as:
questions.explanation: Deuteromycetes are commonly known as imperfect fungi because only the asexual or vegetative phases of these fungi are known.

questions.question: Why are Deuteromycetes called 'imperfect fungi'?

options.B: Only the asexual or vegetative phases are known

questions.explanation: Deuteromycetes are called imperfect fungi because only the asexual or vegetative phases of these fungi are known.

questions.question: Deuteromycetes reproduce only by asexual spores known as:
questions.explanation: Deuteromycetes reproduce only by asexual spores known as conidia.

questions.question: The mycelium of Deuteromycetes is:
questions.explanation: The mycelium of deuteromycetes is septate and branched.

questions.question: A large number of Deuteromycetes are:

options.C: Decomposers of litter and help in mineral cycling

questions.explanation: A large number of deuteromycetes are decomposers of litter and help in mineral cycling.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of Deuteromycetes?
questions.explanation: Alternaria, Colletotrichum and Trichoderma are examples of deuteromycetes.

questions.question: Kingdom Plantae includes all eukaryotic organisms that contain:
questions.explanation: Kingdom Plantae includes all eukaryotic chlorophyll-containing organisms commonly called plants.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of an insectivorous plant?
questions.explanation: Bladderwort and Venus fly trap are examples of insectivorous plants.

questions.question: Cuscuta is an example of a plant that is:
questions.explanation: Cuscuta is a parasite — a partially heterotrophic plant.

questions.question: The cell wall of plant cells is mainly made of:
questions.explanation: The plant cells have an eukaryotic structure with prominent chloroplasts and cell wall mainly made of cellulose.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT included in Kingdom Plantae?
questions.explanation: Plantae includes algae, bryophytes, pteridophytes, gymnosperms and angiosperms, but NOT fungi.

questions.question: The life cycle of plants has two distinct phases. The phenomenon of alternation of these phases is called:
questions.explanation: The life cycle of plants alternates between diploid sporophytic and haploid gametophytic phases. This is called alternation of generations.

questions.question: The two phases in the life cycle of plants are:
questions.explanation: Life cycle of plants has two distinct phases – the diploid sporophytic and the haploid gametophytic – that alternate with each other.

questions.question: Kingdom Animalia is characterised by organisms that are:

options.B: Heterotrophic eukaryotes, multicellular, cells lack cell walls

questions.explanation: Kingdom Animalia is characterised by heterotrophic eukaryotic organisms that are multicellular and their cells lack cell walls.

questions.question: Animals digest their food in:
questions.explanation: Animals digest their food in an internal cavity and store food reserves as glycogen or fat.

questions.question: Food is stored in animals as:
questions.explanation: Animals store food reserves as glycogen or fat.

questions.question: The mode of nutrition in Kingdom Animalia is:
questions.explanation: The mode of nutrition in Kingdom Animalia is holozoic — by ingestion of food.

questions.question: Sexual reproduction in Kingdom Animalia is by:

options.D: Copulation of male and female followed by embryological development

questions.explanation: Sexual reproduction in Kingdom Animalia is by copulation of male and female followed by embryological development.

questions.question: The word 'virus' means:
questions.explanation: Virus means venom or poisonous fluid.

questions.question: Viruses did not find a place in classification because they are:

options.B: Not considered truly living, having no cell structure

questions.explanation: Viruses did not find a place in classification since they are not considered truly 'living', as they lack a cell structure.

questions.question: Viruses are non-cellular organisms characterised by having an inert crystalline structure:
questions.explanation: Viruses are non-cellular organisms characterised by having an inert crystalline structure outside the living cell.

questions.question: Who recognised certain microbes as the causal organism of the mosaic disease of tobacco in 1892?
questions.explanation: Dmitri Ivanowsky (1892) recognised certain microbes as causal organism of the mosaic disease of tobacco.

questions.question: Ivanowsky's microbes passed through bacteria-proof filters, indicating they were:
questions.explanation: These were found to be smaller than bacteria because they passed through bacteria-proof filters.

questions.question: Who demonstrated that the extract of infected tobacco plants could cause infection in healthy plants and named the new pathogen 'virus'?
questions.explanation: M.W. Beijerinck (1898) demonstrated this and named the new pathogen 'virus', calling the fluid Contagium vivum fluidum.

questions.question: Beijerinck called the infectious fluid:
questions.explanation: Beijerinck named the fluid as Contagium vivum fluidum (infectious living fluid).

questions.question: Who showed that viruses could be crystallised and that crystals consist largely of proteins?
questions.explanation: W.M. Stanley (1935) showed that viruses could be crystallised and crystals consist largely of proteins.

questions.question: Viruses are:
questions.explanation: Viruses are obligate parasites — they are inert outside their specific host cell.

questions.question: In addition to proteins, viruses contain:
questions.explanation: Viruses contain genetic material that could be either RNA or DNA. No virus contains both RNA and DNA.

questions.question: Viruses that infect plants generally have:
questions.explanation: In general, viruses that infect plants have single stranded RNA.

questions.question: Viruses that infect animals have:

options.B: Either single or double stranded RNA or double stranded DNA

questions.explanation: Viruses that infect animals have either single or double stranded RNA or double stranded DNA.

questions.question: Bacteriophages (viruses that infect bacteria) are usually:
questions.explanation: Bacterial viruses or bacteriophages are usually double stranded DNA viruses.

questions.question: The protein coat of a virus is called:
questions.explanation: The protein coat called capsid, made of small subunits called capsomeres, protects the nucleic acid.

questions.question: The small subunits of the capsid are called:
questions.explanation: The protein coat called capsid is made of small subunits called capsomeres.

questions.question: Capsomeres are arranged in which geometric forms?
questions.explanation: Capsomeres are arranged in helical or polyhedral geometric forms.

questions.question: Which of the following diseases is caused by a virus?
questions.explanation: Viruses cause diseases like mumps, small pox, herpes and influenza. AIDS in humans is also caused by a virus.

questions.question: AIDS in humans is caused by:
questions.explanation: AIDS in humans is also caused by a virus.

questions.question: Which of the following is a plant viral disease symptom?

options.B: Mosaic formation, leaf rolling and curling, yellowing and vein clearing, dwarfing

questions.explanation: In plants, viral symptoms can be mosaic formation, leaf rolling and curling, yellowing and vein clearing, dwarfing and stunted growth.

questions.question: Who discovered viroids?
questions.explanation: In 1971, T.O. Diener discovered viroids.

questions.question: In which year were viroids discovered?
questions.explanation: In 1971, T.O. Diener discovered a new infectious agent — viroids.

questions.question: What disease did viroids originally cause that led to their discovery?
questions.explanation: T.O. Diener discovered viroids when studying potato spindle tuber disease.

questions.question: Viroids are smaller than viruses and consist of:
questions.explanation: Viroids are free RNA; they lack the protein coat that is found in viruses.

questions.question: The RNA of viroids is of:
questions.explanation: The RNA of the viroid was of low molecular weight.

questions.question: Prions consist of:
questions.explanation: Prions are infectious agents consisting of abnormally folded protein.

questions.question: Prions cause which type of diseases?
questions.explanation: Prions cause certain infectious neurological diseases.

questions.question: Bovine spongiform encephalopathy (BSE) is commonly called:
questions.explanation: Bovine spongiform encephalopathy (BSE) is commonly called mad cow disease in cattle.

questions.question: What is the human equivalent of mad cow disease (BSE)?
questions.explanation: The human analogous variant of BSE is Creutzfeldt–Jacob disease (CJD).

questions.question: Lichens are symbiotic associations between:
questions.explanation: Lichens are symbiotic associations i.e. mutually useful associations, between algae and fungi.

questions.question: The algal component in lichens is known as:
questions.explanation: The algal component in lichens is known as phycobiont.

questions.question: The fungal component in lichens is known as:
questions.explanation: The fungal component in lichens is known as mycobiont.

questions.question: In lichens, what does the algal component (phycobiont) do?
questions.explanation: Algae prepare food for fungi in the lichen association.

questions.question: In lichens, what does the fungal component (mycobiont) do for the algae?

options.C: Provides shelter and absorbs mineral nutrients and water

questions.explanation: Fungi provide shelter and absorb mineral nutrients and water for the algal partner in lichens.

questions.question: Lichens are very good:

options.B: Pollution indicators — they do not grow in polluted areas

questions.explanation: Lichens are very good pollution indicators — they do not grow in polluted areas.

questions.question: Early classification of organisms was done instinctively based on the need to use organisms for:
questions.explanation: Early classification was done instinctively, borne out of a need to use organisms for food, shelter and clothing.

questions.question: Chlamydomonas and Chlorella were earlier placed in:
questions.explanation: Chlamydomonas and Chlorella were earlier placed in Algae within Plants, and both have cell walls.

questions.question: Paramoecium and Amoeba were earlier placed in:
questions.explanation: Paramoecium and Amoeba were earlier placed in the animal kingdom as they lack cell walls.

questions.question: How many bacteria are present in a handful of soil?
questions.explanation: Hundreds of bacteria are present in a handful of soil.

questions.question: Bacteria live in extreme habitats such as:

options.B: Hot springs, deserts, snow and deep oceans

questions.explanation: Bacteria also live in extreme habitats such as hot springs, deserts, snow and deep oceans where very few other life forms can survive.

questions.question: The spores of zoospores produced in phycomycetes are endogenously produced in:
questions.explanation: In phycomycetes, zoospores and aplanospores are endogenously produced in sporangium.

questions.question: Wheat rust is caused by:
questions.explanation: Wheat rust-causing Puccinia is an important example of a disease-causing fungus.

questions.question: Which fungus is the source of antibiotics?
questions.explanation: Some fungi are the source of antibiotics, e.g., Penicillium.

questions.question: Fungi are cosmopolitan and occur in:

options.C: Air, water, soil and on animals and plants

questions.explanation: Fungi are cosmopolitan and occur in air, water, soil and on animals and plants.

questions.question: We keep food in the refrigerator to prevent:
questions.explanation: We keep food in the refrigerator to prevent food from going bad due to bacterial or fungal infections.

questions.question: A virus is described as a:

options.B: Nucleoprotein where the genetic material is infectious

questions.explanation: A virus is a nucleoprotein and the genetic material is infectious.

questions.question: Ivanowsky worked on which plant disease in 1892?
questions.explanation: Dmitri Ivanowsky (1892) recognised certain microbes as causal organism of the mosaic disease of tobacco.

questions.question: In lichen association, the phycobiont is:
questions.explanation: The algal component (phycobiont) is autotrophic in the lichen association.

questions.question: In lichen association, the mycobiont is:
questions.explanation: The fungal component (mycobiont) is heterotrophic in the lichen association.

questions.question: Which kingdom is NOT present in the Whittaker five kingdom classification?
questions.explanation: Viruses are not included in Whittaker's five kingdom classification as they are not considered truly living.

questions.question: Some hyphae of fungi have cross walls called:
questions.explanation: Others have septae or cross walls in their hyphae (as opposed to coenocytic hyphae).

questions.question: Fungi growing on dung are called:
questions.explanation: Ascomycetes may be coprophilous (growing on dung).

questions.question: The cell wall of Monera is made of:
questions.explanation: Monera cell wall is noncellulosic — polysaccharide + amino acid.

questions.question: The protistan cell body contains a well defined nucleus and other:
questions.explanation: Being eukaryotes, the protistan cell body contains a well defined nucleus and other membrane-bound organelles.

questions.question: In diatoms, the cell walls form two thin overlapping shells that fit together as:
questions.explanation: In diatoms the cell walls form two thin overlapping shells, which fit together as in a soap box.

questions.question: The two flagella of dinoflagellates are oriented:

options.C: One longitudinally and the other transversely in a furrow

questions.explanation: Most dinoflagellates have two flagella; one lies longitudinally and the other transversely in a furrow between the wall plates.

questions.question: Pellicle in euglenoids makes their body:
questions.explanation: The pellicle is a protein rich layer that makes their body flexible.

questions.question: Coordinated movement of rows of cilia in ciliated protozoans causes water laden with food to be steered into the:
questions.explanation: The coordinated movement of rows of cilia causes the water laden with food to be steered into the gullet.

questions.question: In Basidiomycetes, asexual spores are generally:
questions.explanation: In basidiomycetes, asexual spores are generally not found, but vegetative reproduction by fragmentation is common.

questions.question: Once perfect (sexual) stages of deuteromycetes were discovered, they were often moved to:
questions.explanation: Once perfect (sexual) stages of members of deuteromycetes were discovered, they were often moved to ascomycetes and basidiomycetes.

questions.question: Bladderwort is an example of:
questions.explanation: Bladderwort and Venus fly trap are examples of insectivorous plants — partially heterotrophic.

questions.question: Higher forms of animals show elaborate:
questions.explanation: Higher forms of animals show elaborate sensory and neuromotor mechanism.

questions.question: Most animals are capable of:
questions.explanation: Most animals are capable of locomotion.

questions.question: Animals follow a definite growth pattern and grow into adults with:
questions.explanation: Animals follow a definite growth pattern and grow into adults that have a definite shape and size.

questions.question: Which kingdom's cell wall has cellulose?
questions.explanation: Kingdom Plantae has a cell wall mainly made of cellulose.

questions.question: Some members of Protista have:
questions.explanation: Some protists have flagella or cilia for movement.

questions.question: Archaebacteria differ from eubacteria in having:
questions.explanation: Archaebacteria differ from other bacteria in having a different cell wall structure and this feature is responsible for their survival in extreme conditions.

questions.question: Cyanobacteria can be unicellular, colonial or:
questions.explanation: Cyanobacteria are unicellular, colonial or filamentous, freshwater/marine or terrestrial algae.

questions.question: Which virus causes mosaic disease in tobacco plants?
questions.explanation: Tobacco Mosaic Virus (TMV) causes mosaic disease of tobacco, first studied by Ivanowsky.

questions.question: The body organisation of Protista is:
questions.explanation: Protista has cellular body organisation.

questions.question: Phycomycetes include which bread mould?
questions.explanation: Mucor and Rhizopus (the bread mould mentioned earlier) are examples of phycomycetes.

questions.question: Aspergillus belongs to which class of fungi?
questions.explanation: Aspergillus is an example of ascomycetes.

questions.question: Trichoderma belongs to which class of fungi?
questions.explanation: Trichoderma is an example of deuteromycetes.

questions.question: Colletotrichum belongs to which class of fungi?
questions.explanation: Colletotrichum is an example of deuteromycetes.

questions.question: Anisogamous condition in phycomycetes means the gametes are:
questions.explanation: When gametes are dissimilar in morphology, it is called anisogamous or oogamous.

questions.question: The dikaryophase in fungi refers to the phase where cells have:
questions.explanation: The dikaryophase is characterised by two nuclei per cell (n + n), called a dikaryon.

questions.question: Bacteria that are helpful in fixing nitrogen in legume roots are:
questions.explanation: Heterotrophic bacteria are helpful in making curd from milk, production of antibiotics, fixing nitrogen in legume roots.

questions.question: Which disease is caused by bacteria?
questions.explanation: Tetanus is a disease caused by bacteria (along with cholera, typhoid, citrus canker).

questions.question: Citrus canker is caused by:
questions.explanation: Citrus canker is a well-known disease caused by different bacteria.

questions.question: The plasmodium of slime moulds may grow and spread over:
questions.explanation: The plasmodium may grow and spread over several feet.

questions.question: All prokaryotic organisms were grouped together under which kingdom in Whittaker's classification?
questions.explanation: All prokaryotic organisms were grouped together under Kingdom Monera.

questions.question: Unicellular eukaryotic organisms were placed in which kingdom?
questions.explanation: Unicellular eukaryotic organisms were placed in Kingdom Protista.

questions.question: Plural of Coccus is:
questions.explanation: The plural of Coccus is cocci.

questions.question: Plural of Bacillus is:
questions.explanation: The plural of Bacillus is bacilli.

questions.question: Plural of Spirillum is:
questions.explanation: The plural of Spirillum is spirilla.

questions.question: Herpes disease is caused by:
questions.explanation: Viruses cause diseases like mumps, small pox, herpes and influenza.

questions.question: Influenza is caused by:
questions.explanation: Influenza is one of the diseases caused by viruses.

questions.question: Small pox is caused by:
questions.explanation: Small pox is caused by a virus.

questions.question: Diatoms are indestructible because their cell walls are:
questions.explanation: The walls of diatoms are embedded with silica and thus the walls are indestructible.

questions.question: The golden algae are also called:
questions.explanation: Chrysophytes include diatoms and golden algae (desmids).

questions.question: White spots on mustard leaves are due to:
questions.explanation: White spots seen on mustard leaves are due to a parasitic fungus (Albugo).

questions.question: Yeast is used to make:
questions.explanation: Some unicellular fungi, e.g., yeast, are used to make bread and beer.

questions.question: Claviceps belongs to which class of fungi?
questions.explanation: Claviceps is an example of ascomycetes.

questions.question: In Basidiomycetes, sexual organs are:
questions.explanation: In basidiomycetes, sex organs are absent, but plasmogamy is brought about by fusion of two vegetative or somatic cells.

questions.question: How do viroids compare to viruses in size?
questions.explanation: Viroids are smaller than viruses — they were discovered as a new infectious agent smaller than viruses.

questions.question: Prions are similar in size to:
questions.explanation: The prion agent was similar in size to viruses.

questions.question: Photosynthetic autotrophic bacteria include:
questions.explanation: Cyanobacteria are photosynthetic autotrophs with chlorophyll a similar to green plants.

questions.question: Protists reproduce asexually and sexually. Sexual reproduction involves:
questions.explanation: Protists reproduce asexually and sexually by a process involving cell fusion and zygote formation.

questions.question: Red tides make the sea appear red because:

options.B: Rapid multiplication of red dinoflagellates like Gonyaulax

questions.explanation: Red dinoflagellates like Gonyaulax undergo rapid multiplication that makes the sea appear red (red tides).

questions.question: The plasmogamy step in fungi involves:

options.C: Fusion of protoplasms between two motile or non-motile gametes

questions.explanation: Plasmogamy involves fusion of protoplasms between two motile or non-motile gametes.

questions.question: What is the body organisation of Kingdom Animalia?
questions.explanation: Kingdom Animalia has tissue/organ/organ system body organisation.

questions.question: What is the body organisation of Kingdom Plantae?
questions.explanation: Kingdom Plantae has tissue/organ body organisation.

questions.question: Typhoid is caused by:
questions.explanation: Typhoid is a well known disease caused by different bacteria.

questions.question: When sunlight is available, Euglenoids are:
questions.explanation: Euglenoids are photosynthetic in the presence of sunlight.

questions.question: Slime mould spores are extremely resistant and can survive for:
questions.explanation: The spores of slime moulds are extremely resistant and survive for many years, even under adverse conditions.

questions.question: Penicillium belongs to which class of fungi?
questions.explanation: Penicillium is an example of ascomycetes (mostly multicellular).

questions.question: Classification criteria that were included beyond gross morphology include:

options.B: Cell structure, nature of wall, mode of nutrition, habitat, methods of reproduction, evolutionary relationships

questions.explanation: A need was felt for including other characteristics like cell structure, nature of wall, mode of nutrition, habitat, methods of reproduction, evolutionary relationships, etc.

questions.question: Fungi that are saprophytic are called so because they:

options.B: Absorb organic matter from dead substrates

questions.explanation: Most fungi are heterotrophic and absorb soluble organic matter from dead substrates and hence are called saprophytes.

questions.question: The genetic material of a virus is:
questions.explanation: Viruses contain genetic material that could be either RNA or DNA. No virus contains both RNA and DNA.

questions.question: Protista cell wall is:
questions.explanation: In Protista, cell wall is present in some members.

questions.question: Which of the following is a type of archaebacteria?
questions.explanation: Methanogens are archaebacteria found in marshy areas and gut of ruminants.

questions.question: Which parameter distinguished fungi from green plants in Whittaker's classification?

options.B: Cell wall composition — chitin in fungi vs cellulose in plants

questions.explanation: Fungi had chitin in their walls while green plants had a cellulosic cell wall — this characteristic distinction led to fungi being placed in a separate kingdom.

questions.question: If you see a lichen in nature, you would:

options.A: Easily identify it as two organisms
options.B: Never imagine it has two different organisms
options.D: See it as a fungus only

questions.explanation: So close is their association that if one saw a lichen in nature, one would never imagine that they had two different organisms within them.

questions.question: In which type of fungi does the sexual reproduction result immediately in diploid cells without any dikaryotic stage?

options.C: Some fungi where fusion of two haploid cells immediately results in diploid cells

questions.explanation: In some fungi the fusion of two haploid cells immediately results in diploid cells (2n). However, in ascomycetes and basidiomycetes, an intervening dikaryotic stage occurs.

questions.question: Amoeboid protozoans can live in:

options.C: Fresh water, sea water or moist soil

questions.explanation: Amoeboid protozoans live in fresh water, sea water or moist soil.

questions.question: Flagellated protozoans are either:
questions.explanation: The members of the flagellated protozoan group are either free-living or parasitic.

questions.question: Boundaries of Kingdom Protista are:
questions.explanation: All single-celled eukaryotes are placed under Protista, but the boundaries of this kingdom are not well defined.

questions.question: Bacteria as a group have simple structure but are:
questions.explanation: Though the bacterial structure is very simple, they are very complex in behaviour.

questions.question: Viruses once they infect a cell:

options.B: Take over the machinery of the host cell to replicate themselves, killing the host

questions.explanation: Once they infect a cell, viruses take over the machinery of the host cell to replicate themselves, killing the host.

questions.question: W.M. Stanley showed that viruses could be crystallised in which year?
questions.explanation: W.M. Stanley (1935) showed that viruses could be crystallised and crystals consist largely of proteins.

questions.question: Plant and animal kingdoms have been a constant under:
questions.explanation: Though plant and animal kingdoms have been a constant under all different systems, the understanding of what groups/organisms be included have been changing.

questions.question: Kingdom Plantae also includes which group of organisms?
questions.explanation: Plantae includes algae, bryophytes, pteridophytes, gymnosperms and angiosperms.

questions.question: Kingdom Plantae includes:
questions.explanation: Kingdom Plantae includes all eukaryotic chlorophyll-containing organisms.

questions.question: Mode of nutrition in Kingdom Fungi is:
questions.explanation: Kingdom Fungi has heterotrophic mode of nutrition — saprophytic/parasitic.

questions.question: Which organisms are absent in the Monera kingdom that are present in all other kingdoms?
questions.explanation: Kingdom Monera organisms (prokaryotes) lack a nuclear membrane, which is present in all other kingdoms (eukaryotes).

questions.question: Most chrysophytes are:
questions.explanation: Most chrysophytes are photosynthetic.

questions.question: Nostoc is an example of which type of organism?
questions.explanation: Nostoc is a cyanobacterium that can fix atmospheric nitrogen in specialised cells called heterocysts.

questions.question: The three steps of sexual cycle in fungi are (in order):
questions.explanation: The sexual cycle in fungi involves: (i) Plasmogamy (ii) Karyogamy (iii) Meiosis in zygote.

questions.question: Animals depend on plants for food:
questions.explanation: Animals directly or indirectly depend on plants for food.

questions.question: What makes viroids different from viruses?

options.A: Viroids have protein coat, viruses don't
options.B: Viroids are free RNA without protein coat; viruses have a protein coat (capsid)
options.D: Viroids can infect animals, viruses cannot

questions.explanation: Viroids are free RNA and lack the protein coat found in viruses. This is the key difference.
